ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for 'Hearty Tomato Noodles' specifically; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta and noodle dishes) used as the closest defensible category for this wheat-noodle-based dish with tomato sauce and chicken. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific Singaporean preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific noodle dish preparation. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared mixed dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Hearty Tomato Noodles is approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as commonly served in Singaporean food courts and casual dining establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Hearty Tomato Noodles — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hearty Tomato Noodles (cr_id G00010642), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous noodle dish data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles / pasta analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Hearty Tomato Noodles' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: wheat noodles (boiled), GI approximately 47–58 (mean ~52), as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The tomato sauce and vegetable components are expected to have a modest GI-lowering effect due to acidity and fibre, supporting the lower end of this range. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (18.72 g/100g × 350 g serving = 65.5 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 34.1 per full serving; per 100 g portion GL = 52 × 18.72 / 100 = 9.7. GL per serving (350 g) = 52 × 65.5 / 100 = 34.1. Reported GL per serving = 34.1 rounded; note: GL per serving (350 g) = 34.1. For consistency with template field 'glycemic_load_per_serving' representing a standard 350 g plate: GL = 34.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 131 mg/100 g — well below the 200 mg/100 g threshold; favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Hearty Tomato Noodles is suitable for individuals with hypertension at 131 mg sodium per 100 g; however, patients should request reduced-sauce or no-added-salt preparation and use fresh or low-sodium tomato sauce to preserve this favourable sodium profile across a full 350 g serving (~459 mg sodium).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodle base with 18.72 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 1.1 g dietary fibre/100 g, yielding an estimated GI of 52 and a high glycemic load per full serving (~34)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, substitute refined wheat noodles with wholemeal, soba (buckwheat), or shirataki noodles to reduce glycemic load, and increase vegetable content to improve fibre-mediated glucose modulation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (6.53 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~1.4 g/100 g; low sodium; tomato lycopene provides antioxidant benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hearty Tomato Noodles presents a low cardiovascular concern profile due to low sodium (131 mg/100 g), moderate total fat, and the antioxidant contribution of lycopene from tomato sauce; patients with CVD should ensure the dish is prepared with unsaturated cooking oil (e.g., canola or olive oil) rather than lard or palm oil, and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato is a nightshade vegetable; solanine content may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive RA individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (solanine) should avoid or substitute the tomato sauce with a non-nightshade alternative such as pumpkin-based sauce, and consider adding anti-inflammatory herbs (turmeric, ginger, basil, oregano) to the preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hearty Tomato Noodles is a Singaporean noodle dish consisting of boiled wheat noodles served in or topped with a tangy tomato-based sauce, accompanied by fresh vegetables, a soft-cooked or hard-boiled egg, and house-marinated chicken breast meat. The dish is a common offering in Singaporean food courts, hawker centres, and casual dining restaurants, typically served as a single-plate lunch or dinner. The flavour profile is tangy and mildly savoury, with the acidity of the tomato sauce balanced by the umami of the marinated chicken and the richness of the egg. The texture combines soft, slightly chewy wheat noodles with tender chicken, yielding vegetables, and a silky sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides 148 kcal per 100 g, with 3.94 g protein, 6.53 g fat, and 18.72 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is modest at 1.1 g/100 g, reflecting the refined wheat noodle base. Sodium is low at 131 mg/100 g, making it suitable for hypertension management at standard portions. The tomato component contributes lycopene, an antioxidant associated with cardiovascular protection. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the refined carbohydrate base and estimated glycemic index of 52 (low GI, analogous to boiled wheat noodles) result in a high glycemic load per full serving (~34 GL for a 350 g plate), warranting portion control or noodle substitution. The dish is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables due to the tomato sauce content.

Is tomato noodles suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010642) with a reported confidence of 0.95 and review status 'reviewed'. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous noodle dish data (USDA FDC ID 174273, pasta with tomato sauce, adapted) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown. This is documented as an adapted value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish (G00010642) and are not available in a directly applicable approved source for this specific Singaporean preparation as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2021 international GI tables); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = 34.1. 4. Halal status is assumed based on the dish description (chicken breast, vegetables, egg, tomato sauce) with no pork or alcohol ingredients indicated; formal halal certification status is not confirmed in the HPB source. 5. The rheumatoid arthritis condition is included in additional_conditions as it was present in the input health_suitability data and is clinically relevant given the nightshade (tomato) content.
